Stolen Milk
Adventure #5—Lights... Camera... The next evening, instead of being assailed with noise as I approached Tom’s garage, I was hit with theChange the Action
delicious smell of hamburgers being grilled.
Adventure #6—The Write Stuff
Adventure #7—Oh, What a Tangled “Welcome to McTom’s,” Tom called out, as I came in the door. “I hope you’re hungry.”	Web We Weave Previous Lesson Next Lesson	Mark as Complete
Adventure #8—To Sleep, Perchance
to Come Up with Great Ideas I noticed that the smell was coming from a pan on a hotplate, topped by a lid with wires coming out of it.
Adventure #9—The Beginning
Postscript... Four Years Later “What do you think?” asked Tom. “See—the cover has heating coils in it, so the food cooks on both sides at
once. Got tired of waiting for my burgers to cook on one side and then having to flip them over and do the whole
process again. Seemed tedious.”
“Makes sense,” I nodded. “How’d you come up with that one?”
“Simple. Found it right there in box number two: Do the Opposite. Food on my hotplate or stove gets heated
from the bottom. I just flipped it around so it also gets heated from the top.”
“Hmmm, and I suppose you’re going to tell me that this box has wider implications than just engineering?”
“Absolutely. Trying to ‘do the opposite’ can take you to some surprisingly creative and effective ideas. Try it.”
I thought for a moment, struggling for ideas. Tom, picking up on this, prompted me further. “What’s something
that you—or the bookselling industry in general—have always done in a certain way?”
“Well, we buy books from the publishers, people visit the store, they look at the books, they pick which ones they
like, they buy them, and they take the books home with them.”
Tom’s eyes lit up. “And what if they didn’t buy them?”
“What?”
“What if they didn’t buy them?” He repeated. “What if we turned that around and said people don’t buy the books
before they take them home. Let’s make it work somehow. Don’t be afraid to be absurd—it may lead you
somewhere brilliant.”
“Okay… people don’t buy the books. Instead, we… we… ok, no, I genuinely don’t see how this one could work.”
“Push! Go past your preconceived notions.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 8 --

“Well…” I racked my brain. “I guess… we could let customers take the books home without paying, to see if they
like them or not. We could… let’s see… we could get their credit card information but not charge it for a week or
so. If they don’t like the book, they return it and we don’t put the charge through. But it would probably never work,
because people would take advantage of it and—”
“No,” Tom interrupted. “Tell me how it could work.”
“Oh… it could work, I suppose, if it increased profits by more than whatever we lost by people forgetting or
refusing to bring the books back, and the card payment being rejected. Come to think of it, people might be a
whole lot more willing to take a book home with them knowing they wouldn’t have to pay for it if they didn’t like it.
And people rarely don’t like a book once they’ve started reading it.”
“As we say in the lab,” said Tom, “maybe it’s worth an experiment. You don’t have to commit to it forever—try it
for a day or two.”
“That seems reasonable. What other ways can we look at it?” I asked, my skin prickling with excitement at all the
ideas I was coming up with.
“Well, there are many things you can do to an object or process besides reversing it. Say, I think the burgers are
ready.” Tom took the two hamburgers out of the pan and put them on buns. Then, he pulled out an old-fashioned
Veg-O-Matic from beneath the worktable.
“Onions?” he asked as he began slicing and dicing an onion. “ Do the Opposite is one of a series of four boxes
from what I call my ‘Veg-O-Matic,’ or ‘slice-and-dice’ boxes. Great invention, by the way, the Veg-O-Matic. So
simple, yet so useful. Anyway, the next ‘slice-and-dice’ box is number three: Divide Something Up .”
Tom began rummaging through one of the cabinets and pulled out a box about the size of a desk drawer. “Do
you ski?” he asked.
“A little. Why?”
“Personally, the thing I always really hated about skiing was lugging skis around—having to attach them to the
top of the car, and then carry them around the lodge and to the slopes. So, I set out to try to solve the problem. I
got to the Divide Something Up box, and bingo!” Tom then pulled out two accordioned Plexiglass strips from
the box, which he proceeded to unfold into a pair of full-sized skis. As each section unfolded, they locked into
place with a reassuring click.
“There you go,” he said, handing them to me. “I wouldn’t use them to compete at the Alpine World Cup, but
they’re sturdy enough for my leisurely meanderings down the mountain.”
Tom pulled out a small bag from the crisper of his mini-fridge. “Carrots?” he asked.
“Sure. I love baby carrots.”
“Actually, these are not baby carrots,” clarified Tom. Most people think these little carrots are specially grown
miniature carrots. But what they are is a great example of ‘dividing it up.’
“The story is that a few years ago, the owner of a carrot processing plant in California had a problem. He had to
discard tons of misshapen carrots every day. So he came up with the idea of dividing these unwanted carrots
into small 2” pieces, rolling them around to smooth them up nicely, and voila—‘baby’ carrots were born and
caught on like wildfire.”
“Now,” he continued, excitedly, “how might we slice and dice your business for fun and profit? Inside this box, you
can either divide something into separate parts or sections or—if it’s already in sections—you can divide it up
even more.”
“That might have some implications for my business. Of course, a bookstore is divided into ‘sections.’ So, I
could increase the division by upping the number of sections, which might make it easier for people to find
books on their specific interests.”
“Good,” said Tom. “Now, what’s not sectional that you could improve by dividing it more?”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 8 --

“Let’s see… How about this: We’ve been selling quite a few books online lately. I bet that part of our business
would really take off if more energy were focused on it. Maybe I could make that into a separate ‘section’ or
profit center and let Robert devote himself full time to it.
“Same with our ‘institutional’ business, where we sell books to companies in bulk. I could block that off into a
separate profit center and put Nancy in charge of it. I bet it could really make a difference if we had devoted
attention to these sectors. Does that count as ‘dividing it up?’”
“It sure does—but don’t worry about that. As long as you’re coming up with ideas, that’s all that matters.”
“Yeah, I guess you’re right. This is fun. Let’s keep going!”
“Okay,” Tom replied with a grin. “Give me another problem to solve that would help get your bookstore back on
its feet.”
“Shoplifters,” I blurted out. “I lose a small fortune to them every year.”
“Good. Let’s look at how we might solve that problem with box number four: Take Something Out . What can you
remove from an object or process that’s keeping you from getting the results you want? Or, how can you extract
only that part or characteristic you really want from an object or process?”
“Take the shoplifters out and shoot them?” I replied—only half-joking.
“Excellent,” laughed Tom. “Humor is an essential part of the creative process. Loosens up the mind. Creates
unexpected connections. Makes the process more enjoyable. Even such a seemingly off-the-cuff remark could
lead to some good ideas.”
“Like what?” I asked eagerly.
“Well, it could lead to ideas on how you might prosecute shoplifters more vigorously. Or, perhaps you really
should put up a sign that reads ‘Shoplifters will be shot.’ If nothing else, it’s sure to grab attention.”
“Gosh, you don’t waste an idea, do you?”
“Nope. Anything—absolutely anything —can lead you to a good idea. How else might you ‘take out’ the
shoplifters? And remember: Sometimes, things are ‘taken out’ by never being allowed in in the first place.”
“Hey, here’s an idea. What if my bookstore could be ‘by invitation only?’ So you have to be invited to get in. That
would keep out the shoplifters and lend a certain exclusivity that bigger bookstores could never offer.”
“Now you’re talking,” Tom said. “Maybe you could try it on a day or evening you’re not normally open and see
how it works. Start with an ‘invitation-only’ event and, if it works well, you can consider moving toward an
invitation-only or membership-based model.”
“Kind of like building a prototype, right?”
“Exactly. Now you’re starting to think like an engineer.”
“Amazing. What’s the next box?” I asked, not wanting to waste a moment with my new mentor.
“Here’s another one for you. We’ve divided things up and taken things out. Now, let’s combine things in box
number five: Bring Some Things Together . In fact, this box spurred one of my favorite stories about someone
using my ideas system.
“Oh? Tell me more?” I insisted.
“A friend of mine runs a nursing home. I showed him how to ‘think inside the box,’ like I’m showing you. He had a
problem with two of his more senile residents. One woman kept grabbing food off other people’s plates, and it
was driving her fellow diners crazy. The other, a man, thought people were poisoning his food, and his
accusations were likewise causing quite a commotion.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 8 --

“My friend looked in the various boxes. He was almost ready to use the first box and separate these two
troublemakers from the rest of the residents during meals. But then, he looked in this box, Bring Some Things
Together , and… what do you think he did?”
“Put them together?” I guessed the only logical answer.
“Yes, and he did it in an absolutely brilliant way. Before seating them together at meals, he told the man who
thought his food was being poisoned that the woman had volunteered to be his ‘food taster’ and to make sure
his food was safe.
“He was happy to have her test his food periodically. And now that the woman could take food from someone
else without causing an uproar, so she stopped bothering the other residents.
“We inventors are always putting this together with that, taking a doodad from here and matching it with a
doohickey from there.
“Ben Franklin combined reading glasses with long distance glasses and came up with bifocals. Apple
combined downloading music onto a computer hard drive with portability and headphones and came up with the
iPod. It’s a great way to come up with new products or services—or new ways to market yourself.
“Yet businesses tend to operate in a very isolated, non-combinational way. For example, why can’t you and the
shop next door combine your efforts and do a joint promotion? What do they sell?”
“It’s a jewelry store, so it wouldn’t work because—”
“Remember,” interrupted Tom, “no pessimism—only what is possible. What if you ran an ad that read: ‘Beautify
her body and her mind? Show her you love her for both. This week only—Buy a piece of jewelry and get your
choice of any book.’”
“That’s good ,” I said, scribbling it down on a scrap of paper.
“Don’t worry about writing everything down,” Tom reassured me. “What’s important is the boxes and getting into
the habit of using them. If you do, believe me, you’ll soon be generating ideas faster than you can use them.”
“I am already!” I said. “I could team up with all sorts of businesses by advertising with them and maybe even
sending out promotions to their mailing lists. Putting things together makes me think about how I could take
advantage of current events by displaying books according to topics currently in the news. I could also—”
Tom interrupted again, “I think you’ve got this box down pat. Why don’t you stop by again tomorrow, and I’ll tell
you what you can learn from the matryoshka .”
“Is that a box?”
“Yes. And a doll.”
“Huh?”
“You’ll see.”
Summary & Exercises
In a Nutshell
Let’s get right down to play—playing around with ideas, that is.
Why “play”? Because nothing makes it harder to come up with good ideas than a serious environment. If you
think a situation is serious, you’ll be reluctant to say (or write) something wrong and look stupid.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 8 --

But when coming up with ideas is playful—when you’re joking around and having a good time—there’s nothing
to be afraid of. And it isn’t possible to look “stupid”—to yourself or anyone else. This gives you the freedom to
come up with all sorts of interesting possibilities. And that’s the essence of creativity (and what these techniques
help you do).
Which leads to a central idea in Adventure #2: Instead of “Just Do It,” just try it. Just try implementing your idea
or solution. It just might work. (Or just try thinking through how it could possibly be implemented.)
And whether or not it works, you’ll learn a heck of a lot in the attempt. And you’ll understand some things that will
make your next attempt a whole lot more likely to succeed than if you never got your hands dirty.
The Slice and Dice boxes in this Adventure help you take the problem or opportunity you’re working on and
rearrange it in various ways, in order to understand the problem and look at it in different ways, as well as to
generate tons of new ideas.
Let’s take a look at these boxes and have some fun inside them…
Click here to download this fillable PDF
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 8 --

-- 7 of 8 --

Your Action Steps
1. Are there ideas or solutions you have been reluctant to try? Pick one and test it out . See what happens.
MODULES
2. Think of ideas that have worked out well—your own or those of others. What was the basic, underlying
creative technique involved? Did any of them use any of the Slice and Dice boxes? (By the way, you can apply
this “post-mortem” technique to any of the 15 boxes. It’s a great way to understand more about them.)
The next Adventure is one of my favorites—and one of the most important—in the book. There’s one single
concept in it that, all by itself, could change your life.
Try and figure out what it is as you read or watch Adventure #3, if you haven’t already.
Don’t be afraid to be funny or absurd—it may lead you someplace brilliant.
Try it out (build a prototype).
All of these resources are also available in your Idea Power Mindbook if you prefer to write
them out by hand.
